# Connecting AI to Architecture Tools {#sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"We shape our tools and thereafter they shape us."*
>
> — John Culkin, *Saturday Review* (1967)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** John Culkin, an influential American media scholar, observed that tools reshape how people work. An architecture tool also shapes what an AI method can change and what an architect can observe. Those limits must be explicit when the method acts through the tool.
:::


```{=latex}
\abstract*{An AI method needs more than permission to call an architecture tool. The tool path must expose legal actions, returned observations, cost, provenance, constraints, and four distinct outcome paths. This chapter develops that environment contract across ISA boundaries, compiler IRs, memory models, simulator APIs, profilers, RTL flows, EDA handoffs, runtimes, benchmarks, and telemetry systems.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
What must architecture tools expose so AI can use them correctly and another
architect can understand the returned results?
:::

An explicit design representation does not make a tool flow usable. Consider
an AI method evaluating a larger L2 cache with gem5, a full-system architecture
simulator [@BinkertEtAl2011Gem5]. The method needs more than a command that
starts the simulator. It needs to know which
cache parameters it may change, which combinations are legal, which workload
and warm-up rules are fixed, how long a run costs, what warnings mean, and
which result would rule out the candidate. Another architect needs the same
information to judge the comparison.

An experienced architect may carry much of this knowledge without writing it
down. An AI method cannot rely on that private knowledge. A *tool* performs an
operation. A *wrapper* translates a permitted design action into a tool call,
then translates the result into recorded observations and outcome statuses.
Neither one alone states the conditions under which that result can support an
architectural claim.

> **Architecture environment.** An architecture environment is a tool flow
> governed by an explicit contract. That contract specifies the legal actions
> and fixed context, the meaning, cost, and fidelity of returned observations,
> the required provenance and integrity information, and distinct outcome and
> review paths.

The bounded study covers the architecture question, comparison, and intended
decision. Its *environment contract* covers the tool flow. A *harness* is the
maintained runnable implementation of that contract, including workloads,
versions, scripts, provenance, outcome records, and review decisions. These
fields let a method choose legal actions and let a reviewer determine what the
returned feedback can support.

A scoped consequence model and an architecture environment answer different
questions. The model predicts or constrains what may happen after an action.
The environment governs the actual tool interaction and records what happens.
A simulator, surrogate, or rule set can contribute to both. The architect must
identify whether each output is a prediction, a constraint, or an observation
from the tool path before using it to support a claim.

For the L2 example, the contract would name the cache fields that may change,
the fixed workload and warm-up rules, the units and aggregation of each
reported metric, and the cost of a run. It would also distinguish an illegal
configuration, a valid candidate that violates a declared limit, a failed tool
path, and a result that requires review. The wrapper applies those rules when it
calls gem5. The harness retains the inputs, tool version, raw results, and
outcome status. The AI method can then see what it may request, and another
architect can tell what the returned miss count means.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can turn a tool path into a documented environment
contract. That means you can:

- **Turn** a tool into an environment with specified actions, observations, costs, and four outcome statuses;
- **Interpret** a result from its units, aggregation, normalization, uncertainty, scope, and source;
- **Explain** why environment contracts make AI-method comparisons meaningful;
- **Distinguish** illegal requests, evaluated candidates that fail a declared condition, broken tool paths, and unsupported or inconclusive results;
- **Detect** proxy mismatch and proxy gaming, simulators included, before they mislead a method or reviewer;
- **Maintain** a comparable harness as workloads, tools, and action schemas change.
:::

## Tools Shape the Research Question

Computer architects have always used tools to reason quantitatively about
systems. Simulators, analytic models, profilers, compilers, RTL generators,
EDA flows, and measurement systems make design spaces tractable. They also
shape the questions that can be asked. A simulator with a particular memory
model makes some cache questions natural and others awkward. A compiler pass
that provides one schedule representation and hides another constrains what an
optimizer can change. An EDA flow that returns timing and power after hours of
work makes feedback precious. A runtime telemetry system that reports
aggregate utilization but not per-request interference makes some deployment
claims difficult to support.
When an architect operates the tools directly, some of these limits can remain
tacit. An AI method cannot recover a hidden constraint, cost, or rejection rule
from a scalar score. The environment must make those conditions available
before the method acts.

Calling these conditions tool limitations understates their effect. They
determine what an architect can observe and therefore what an architectural
claim can cover. The classic quantitative tradition in computer
architecture shifted the field from intuition to rigorous simulation and
measurement [@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach]. Architecture 2.0
extends that discipline to the measurement interface itself. Inputs, outputs,
assumptions, and unsupported cases all affect what a result means.

An AI method can act only on the interface state it receives. An architect can
sometimes infer that a simulator result is
out of distribution, that a benchmark run used a stale configuration, or that
a reported improvement is not meaningful because the compiler changed. A
method cannot use those facts unless the wrapper exposes them. It needs enough
state to choose a useful action and enough constraints to recognize an invalid
one.

Naming gem5, FireSim, or a commercial EDA tool is not enough. A
reviewer also needs to know which actions the wrapper permits, what the tool
returns, which states are invalid, and which runs or results can be
reproduced. Those details determine whether two method results are actually
comparable.

## What an Architecture Environment Must Expose

The contract connects a permitted design action to a tool result whose meaning
is explicit. It names the architecture question and workload, the state that
may change, the state that must remain fixed, the observations the tool can
return, the cost and fidelity of obtaining them, and the possible outcomes of
the call. @tbl-environment-contract collects those requirements.

| **Field** | **What it must expose** | **Why another architect needs it** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Workload and fixed context** | Architecture question, inputs, traces, benchmark and software versions, operating conditions, and choices held constant. | Shows which behavior the result describes and whether two runs used the same context. |
| **Action schema** | Parameters, edits, configurations, generated artifacts, or commands an AI method may propose. | Defines exactly what the method can change. |
| **Constraints and checks** | Type and range checks, legal combinations, software-visible contracts, physical limits, and checks applied before and after a tool call. | Separates an illegal request from a candidate that was evaluated and then failed a declared condition. |
| **Observation schema** | Metrics, traces, logs, reports, errors, status values, and artifacts returned by the tool path. | Identifies the raw result and the source from which it came. |
| **Observation meaning** | Units, aggregation rule, denominator, normalization, preferred direction, uncertainty or run-to-run variation, and valid scope. | Lets a reviewer interpret a value rather than infer what a metric name means. |
| **Evaluation cost and fidelity** | Wall time, compute, license or hardware use, engineering effort, model stage, determinism, and practical sample limit. | Shows which comparisons are affordable and which observations can be compared. |
| **Provenance and integrity** | Tool, model, prompt, dependency, dataset, host, seed, configuration, artifact identifiers, hashes, and signatures when available. | Shows which inputs and software produced the result and whether an evidence source changed. |
| **Outcome and review paths** | Status categories, retry and rejection rules, escalation conditions, and the architect, reviewer, or decision owner responsible for unresolved cases. | Determines what happens after each result without giving the tool decision authority. |

: **An environment contract defines tool use before execution.** It tells an AI method what it may request and lets another architect interpret the returned result. It does not decide which architectural claim the result supports. {#tbl-environment-contract tbl-colwidths="[22,38,30]"}

A metric name alone is not enough. If gem5 reports cache misses, the contract
must say whether the value is a count or rate, which requests form the
denominator, whether warm-up traffic was removed, whether results are per core
or aggregated, which direction is preferred, and how much the value varies
across repeated runs. Without that information, an optimizer can rank numbers
that another architect cannot interpret.

The outcome status also matters. Four negative outcomes require different
responses.

- **Illegal before execution:** The proposed action violates the schema or a
  condition checked before the tool runs. The wrapper rejects the request
  without treating it as an evaluated design result.
- **Candidate rejected after evaluation:** The tool returns a valid
  observation, but the candidate violates a declared constraint or fails the
  comparison rule. The result remains attached to the candidate.
- **Tool-path failure:** The setup, wrapper, harness, license, or tool fails to
  produce a valid observation. The harness records the failure and its cause
  rather than assigning the result to the candidate by default.
- **Unsupported or inconclusive:** The tool returns an output that falls outside
  its declared scope, or the available checks do not settle the candidate. A
  named architect or reviewer decides what additional tool run or review is
  needed.

These distinctions determine whether the next step is to repair a request,
reject a candidate, repair the tool path, or seek stronger feedback. The
wrapper implements the distinctions, and the harness preserves the inputs,
outputs, status, and provenance. Neither one decides what the architectural
claim should be.

A completed SCALE-Sim comparison provides a concrete example. Its action schema
admitted only legal array geometries. SCALE-Sim returned cycle count and average
layer utilization for accepted inputs, and the harness retained the raw
outputs, status, elapsed time, simulator configuration, and candidate source.
The fixed meanings and equal budgets let another architect interpret the
resulting tie. They did not give the simulator or the proposal method authority
to decide whether the baseline should advance.

## From Tool Request to Review Record

The wrapper and harness make the environment contract executable.
@fig-environment-tool-interface separates the information supplied before a
tool call from the observations and status returned afterward. The center shows
the wrapper checking, calling, and parsing while the harness records the run.

![**A tool becomes an environment when its interface is specified.** The contract fixes permitted actions and observation meaning before execution. The wrapper and harness then keep illegal requests, candidates rejected after evaluation, tool-path failures, and unsupported or inconclusive results distinct while preserving every path in a review record.](images/F6-environment-tool-interface){#fig-environment-tool-interface width="100%" fig-alt="Diagram of an architecture question and workload, action schema, constraints, and metric definitions entering an environment boundary. A wrapper checks the request, calls the tool, and parses the observation and status; a harness records the run and provenance. Separate branches show illegal before execution, tool-path failure, candidate rejected after evaluation, and unsupported or inconclusive pending named review. Raw tool output and every negative outcome feed a review record containing result meaning, cost, fidelity, provenance, and status."}

On the left of @fig-environment-tool-interface, the architecture question and
workload establish the inputs, version, and fixed context. The action schema
names the knobs, edits, or commands an AI method may propose. The constraints
and metric definitions state which requests are legal and how returned values
should be read.

Inside the environment boundary, the wrapper checks the request, invokes the
tool, and parses its observations and status. The harness records the run and
its provenance. An illegal request stops before execution. After a valid tool
run, a declared check may keep the candidate in the comparison or reject it.
A broken setup, harness, or tool produces a tool-path failure instead. An
unsupported or inconclusive result goes to a named architect or reviewer.

The right side separates raw tool output, such as metrics, logs, and reports,
from the review record that preserves those outputs, their meaning, cost,
fidelity, provenance, and status. The figure also routes every negative outcome
into that record. An optimizer may receive one scalar objective, while a
reviewer can still inspect the underlying cycle counts, warnings,
configuration, and tool version. The record supports review but does not
authorize the next architecture decision. Two projects can use the same
simulator while defining different architecture environments because their
actions, workloads, constraints, and observation meanings differ.

The harness should retain the underlying observations and cost data even when a
method receives only a scalar objective. Otherwise, the method may exploit a
simulator bug or omitted cost instead of improving the design.
[^fn-ml-reward-hacking]

[^fn-ml-reward-hacking]: The proxy-gaming section later in this chapter defines reward hacking in full, with the simulator case and its machine-learning-safety source.

## Interfaces Define What AI Can Change

A tool's interface makes its action and observation schema concrete.
Architecture is often described through boundaries between hardware and
software. For an AI system to work through those boundaries, the architect
must spell out what each interface permits, what observations it returns, and
which failures it can detect. This applies to ISA boundaries, compiler IRs,
memory models, runtime APIs, simulator interfaces, benchmark harnesses, EDA
handoffs, and telemetry schemas. When the claim depends on coherence,
scheduling, memory semantics, or physical constraints, the method should act
through the interface or schema that defines those semantics. Unstructured C++
or RTL becomes a review risk when it bypasses those modular interfaces, not
because it is invalid by default.

Tool interfaces belong in the architecture argument rather than in
an appendix. A generator that emits a schedule must use the schedule language.
A search method that changes memory hierarchy parameters must know which
combinations the simulator accepts and which violate a software-visible
contract. A critic that reads a synthesis report must distinguish a fatal
warning from an informational one and know when a higher-fidelity check is
required. The interface connects an AI method's permitted actions to the
architectural rules that govern them.

An AI-assisted architecture comparison may depend on several interfaces
(@tbl-architecture-interface-boundaries). Each interface defines what the
method may do and how an architect should read the tool output. If either
remains implicit, an optimizer may change something the architect meant to
hold fixed or treat a tool result as support for a claim the tool cannot
answer.

| **Interface** | **What the AI method may do** | **What the tool returns and a reviewer can check** | **Risk if hidden** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| ISA and vector contract | Instructions, registers, vector length, exceptions, privilege, and binary compatibility. | Correct execution, portability, software-visible behavior, and compatibility tests. | An AI method proposes a microarchitecture that software cannot legally target. |
| Compiler IR and schedule representation | Lowering choices, tiling, fusion, layout, vectorization, and target-specific code generation. | Compiler success, generated code, performance counters, and optimization provenance. | Performance is attributed to hardware while the software contract changed. |
| Memory and coherence model | Ordering, sharing, cacheability, consistency, DMA, and synchronization assumptions. | Correctness tests, contention behavior, latency, bandwidth, and race or ordering failures. | A candidate looks fast because it violated the program's memory assumptions. |
| Accelerator or runtime API | Invocation, data movement, synchronization, library calls, queues, and resource ownership. | End-to-end latency, overhead, utilization, portability, and software integration cost. | Specialized hardware is efficient in isolation but unusable in the system. |
| Simulator or environment API | Legal parameters, workload inputs, observations, errors, seeds, and fidelity levels. | Run observations, errors, seeds, invalid-action records, elapsed cost, and the metadata needed to check comparability. | The method optimizes simulator quirks or incomparable configurations. |
| EDA handoff and constraints | RTL, clocks, floorplan hints, timing constraints, power intent, physical limits, and signoff checks. | Timing, area, power, congestion, rule violations, and implementation feasibility. | A candidate survives architectural simulation but fails physical reality. |
| Benchmark harness | Inputs, versions, metrics, splits, data-leakage rules, and submission constraints. | Scores, per-case results, coverage metadata, and the version and split information needed to check leakage or repeat the run. | An AI method overfits a stale or leaky benchmark slice. |
| Telemetry and deployment schema | Live workload mix, service-level objectives (SLOs), counters, interference, rollout state, and drift signals. | Field behavior, regressions, rollback triggers, and post-deployment calibration. | Production telemetry is rich but too confounded to support the architecture claim. |

: **Architecture interfaces define what a method can do and what reviewers can inspect.** Each interface states what an AI method may change, what the tool returns, and which checks flag an invalid action or a result that does not support the claim. {#tbl-architecture-interface-boundaries tbl-colwidths="[20,25,25,20]"}

The interfaces in @tbl-architecture-interface-boundaries rarely operate alone.
Suppose an AI method proposes a wider vector unit. The ISA contract determines
whether software can target it, the compiler and runtime determine whether a
workload uses it, the simulator estimates its performance, and the EDA handoff
tests whether the added datapaths can meet timing and power limits. A gain at
one interface does not excuse a failure at another. The environment must keep
the actions and observations from those tool stages connected so a reviewer
can see where the claim holds and where it fails.

### EDA Handoffs and Closed Flows

The EDA handoff row is worth making concrete because its wrapper must translate
between proposed changes and several tool stages. Three checks occur at
different times. Before execution, schema checks ask whether the parameters,
clocks, and interfaces are well formed. After execution, physical checks ask
whether the candidate is routable and can meet timing, congestion, power, and
IR-drop limits. Separately, lifecycle checks ask whether the harness still
tests the intended architecture question after tools, workloads, or interfaces
change. A late physical failure belongs to the evaluated candidate; it is not
an illegal tool request.

Wrapping a commercial flow requires more than a thin API. The wrapper has to
emit tool-specific scripts, parse unstructured reports, survive
timeouts and run-to-run nondeterminism. The architect defines the fidelity label
for each tool stage, and the wrapper records it with the run. The tool itself
emits metrics, warnings, and logs, not a judgment about what those results can
support. To stay auditable, the provenance record must include more than a bare
number. It records the tool and host version, thread count, license state,
run-to-run variance, warnings and waivers, and a hash of the report the number
came from. Two "identical" runs that differ on any of these should not be treated
as comparable until the architect shows that the difference does not affect the
result. A wrapper that hides the difference can make incompatible runs look
like a fair comparison.

Commercial tool and foundry constraints also limit what Architecture 2.0 should
try to standardize. The field does not need a universal vendor API or an open
substitute for every foundry rule. It needs a stable way for reviewers to see
what changed, what was observed, what stayed confidential, and what could rule
out the claim, while leaving proprietary implementation details protected
(@tbl-standardizable-proprietary-contract).

| **Contract layer** | **Can be standardized** | **Usually remains proprietary** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Action schema | Task identifiers, legal edit classes, invalid-action categories, and stage labels. | Vendor command syntax, internal scripts, and product-specific flow recipes. |
| Observation schema | Metric names, fidelity labels, report hashes, warning classes, and failure categories. | Full report bodies, foundry rule details, and confidential workload traces. |
| Run record | Tool/version hashes, seed records, queue or license state, elapsed time, and artifact pointers. | License-server behavior, internal tool telemetry, and nonpublic debug artifacts. |
| Checks and review | Rejection check, reviewer role, conditions requiring review, waiver status, and the action or claim a failure blocks. | Product review details, customer commitments, waiver rationale, and named approvers. |
| Disclosure boundary | Redaction reason, disclosure tier, reviewer role, decisions the reviewer may make, and artifact hash or signature. | Raw PDK contents, proprietary RTL, private baselines, and customer data. |

: **Review records for closed tool flows.** Commercial EDA and foundry flows can use common record fields and review rules without disclosing vendor implementations, foundry rules, signoff details, or protected design state. {#tbl-standardizable-proprietary-contract tbl-colwidths="[18,34,38]"}

Across all five layers, schemas and review records can be standardized while
tool commands, protected data, and implementation details remain private.

### Tool Paths at Different Fidelity

Architecture tools expose these interfaces at different fidelity levels.
Architecture 2.0 does not require one canonical simulator or vendor
flow. The architect must identify the tool path, the observations it can
return, their cost and fidelity, and the later checks that can overturn an early
ranking
(@tbl-environment-instances).

| **Environment instance** | **Permitted actions** | **Returned feedback and records** | **Implication for AI-assisted use** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Cycle-level or full-system simulation (e.g., gem5) [@BinkertEtAl2011Gem5] | Core, cache, memory-system, ISA, and workload configuration. | Statistics, traces, timing-model behavior, simulator warnings, and scoped performance comparisons. | Strong for controlled DSE; bounded by model fidelity and configuration state. |
| Compiled RTL simulation (e.g., Verilator) [@Veripool2026Verilator] | RTL modules, test benches, assertions, generated C++/SystemC models, and debug hooks. | Functional behavior, waveforms, debug logs, assertion failures, and implementation-adjacent traces. | Moves closer to implementation but narrows throughput and increases debug cost. |
| FPGA-accelerated simulation (e.g., FireSim) [@KarandikarEtAl2018FireSim] | RTL target, workload image, network model, FPGA mapping, and runtime configuration. | Faster cycle-exact feedback, workload-scale behavior, instrumented counters, and deployment-like experiments. | Speed changes sample economics, but the architect still needs setup and observability records to interpret the results. |
| Synthesis, place-and-route, and signoff flow [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @SemiconductorIndustryAssociation2026ChipDesign; @BauerEtAl2020SemiconductorDesignManufacturing] | RTL, constraints, floorplan hints, clocks, power intent, libraries, and process assumptions. | Area, timing, power, congestion, rule violations, waived warnings, and closure failures. | Physical runs are scarce; use them selectively to test whether candidates that looked promising under cheaper tools still meet physical constraints. |

: **Tool-specific environment records.** For each comparison, record the simulator, RTL flow, emulation path, EDA stage, or deployment harness because each provides different actions, observations, costs, and outcome paths. {#tbl-environment-instances tbl-colwidths="[22,24,26,18]"}

For review, the tool name matters less than the record of permitted edits,
workloads, results, outcome statuses, and who is responsible for interpreting
them.

In practice these environments are rarely used in isolation. Open frameworks
integrate them. Chipyard, an open-source framework for generating and evaluating
RISC-V systems-on-chip, connects a configuration system, Chisel-based RTL
generators such as the Rocket and BOOM cores and the Hwacha vector unit, and a
FIRRTL compiler path.
It ties these to simulation, synthesis, and FPGA-emulation backends, so each
evaluation begins from the same configuration (@fig-chipyard-framework)
[@chipyard]. An optimizer edits a validated configuration. Simulation,
synthesis, and FPGA emulation then evaluate that configuration, while the
harness records the output and outcome status from each backend. A named
reviewer assesses each result, and the decision owner decides whether it
justifies further work.

![**An integrated environment ties the backends together:** A reproducible SoC generation framework connects a configuration system, RTL generators, and a compiler to simulation, synthesis, and FPGA-emulation backends, so one configuration reaches every evaluation path.](images/F6b-chipyard-framework){#fig-chipyard-framework width="100%" fig-alt="Flow diagram in which a configuration system feeds Chisel RTL generators (Rocket, BOOM, Hwacha) and a FIRRTL compiler, which fan out to RTL simulation, VLSI synthesis, and FPGA emulation backends."}

Read @fig-chipyard-framework from the configuration on the left. Its parameters
drive the Chisel generators and FIRRTL compiler, which produce the RTL consumed
by three evaluation paths. RTL simulation can expose functional and
microarchitectural behavior, the VLSI flow can expose implementation limits,
and FPGA emulation can run larger workloads. Sending one validated
configuration to all three paths keeps the candidate identity stable while the
returned observations and findings change. The framework supplies that
connection, but an environment contract must still record the workload,
backend versions, constraints, costs, warnings, and outcome status for each
path.

The three paths also show why an integrated framework does not collapse
fidelity differences. Post-synthesis timing, for
example, is often a useful surrogate for post-route timing, but routing
congestion, IR drop, clocking, and signoff checks can still reject a candidate
that looked acceptable at synthesis. An architect can begin with a cheaper tool,
but must identify which output is only a proxy and which later check can overturn
it. The environment contract should state what happens after each
higher-fidelity result and who decides whether another evaluation or review is
warranted or whether the candidate should leave the comparison.

Physical-design environments make this staged contract particularly clear. A
wrapper should not report "EDA feedback" as if every tool stage returned the
same kind of result. Logic synthesis, placement, routing, and signoff expose
different candidate problems. The architect must state whether each finding
leads to another tool run, meets a declared rejection condition, or requires
review (@tbl-eda-stage-contract).

| **EDA stage** | **Feedback returned** | **Problem it can reveal** | **What the result cannot establish alone** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Logic synthesis | Mapped gates, area proxy, timing estimates, constraint warnings, and early power estimates. | Nonsynthesizable RTL, impossible constraints, obvious area/timing problems. | Routability, final timing, IR drop, clocking, and post-layout power. |
| Floorplanning and placement | Cell locations, utilization, congestion hints, timing pressure, and macro or memory placement effects. | Floorplans that create severe congestion, timing pressure, or integration problems. | Final routed timing, signoff power, manufacturability, and workload-level benefit. |
| Clock, routing, and power closure | Routed timing, clock behavior, congestion, design-rule checks, power integrity, and closure failures. | Physical effects that invalidate earlier architecture estimates. | Product commitment without verification, workload coverage, and architecture review. |
| Signoff and review | Tool signoff reports, waivers, residual risks, review decisions, and ownership records. | Unsupported claims, unacceptable waived warnings, and evidence gaps before commitment. | Generality beyond the stated workload, process, tool version, and design context. |

: **EDA stages return different findings.** From logic synthesis through
signoff, each physical-design stage can reveal different candidate problems.
The environment records the stage, and the architect declares how each finding
affects the claim. {#tbl-eda-stage-contract tbl-colwidths="[18,26,25,21]"}

Some commercial physical-implementation flows already search synthesis
directives and floorplan parameters, then
evaluate each choice with a full tool run [@Synopsys2023DSOai;
@Cadence2021Cerebrus]. To use such a flow in an AI-assisted comparison, the
wrapper must define the legal directives, preserve the reports and tool
versions, and identify which closure or signoff failures meet a declared
rejection condition. The contract must also name who reviews the result and
who owns the resulting decision.

## ArchGym as a Worked Example

ArchGym, a project from the author's group, shows these requirements in the
architecture domain. It organizes bounded architecture tasks as gym-style
environments with defined actions and observations
[@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. It adopts the agent-environment interface from
OpenAI Gym. An ArchGym user can swap a memory-controller simulator for an
accelerator simulator while retaining the same optimization or learning
method. The Architecture 2.0 gymnasium essay makes a related case for shared
datasets, tasks, tools, and evaluation environments
[@ReddiYazdanbakhsh2023Gymnasium]. A community workshop later broadened that
agenda to include infrastructure, training, best practices, trust, and industry collaboration
[@Architecture20CommunityWorkshop; @Architecture20SIGARCHWorkshopOutcomes].

ArchGym illustrates how a shared environment changes the research question.
Instead of asking whether one optimizer beat another under a private script, the
community can ask which task was defined, which action space was exposed, what
feedback was available, what workloads were used, and which tools or methods
were compared under the same conditions. @fig-ch05-archgym-equal-budget shows
why the simulator-sample budget has to be part of that comparison. It places
five search families inside the same DRAM memory-controller environment and
varies only how many simulator evaluations each family can spend
[@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. Inspect how the apparent ranking changes from the
smallest budgets to the largest rather than looking for one overall winner.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch05-archgym-equal-budget
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Rankings under equal evaluation budgets.** Mean normalized reward (higher is better) for five search families inside the same ArchGym DRAM memory-controller environment at equal simulator-sample budgets [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. Values and whisker extents are digitized transcriptions of the paper's Figure 7(a), not raw result files. The discussion surrounding the plot does not state the normalization reference, aggregation population, or whisker statistic. Intervals overlap across families at every budget, and reinforcement learning has no bar at the two smallest budgets in the source. Read the plot as a budget-dependent comparison, not a significance test or a precise ranking.
#| out-width: "88%"
#| fig-alt: "Grouped bar chart with whiskers showing digitized mean normalized reward for five search families, ACO, BO, GA, RL, and RW, at simulator-sample budgets from one hundred to two hundred fifty thousand in the ArchGym DRAM memory-controller environment. Whisker intervals overlap across families at every budget. Reinforcement learning has no source bar at the two smallest budgets, is near zero at ten thousand samples, and has the highest digitized mean only at the largest budget."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, apply_style

apply_style()

# Source receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter5-archgym-equal-budget.csv
# ArchGym ISCA 2023 [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym], Fig. 7(a): mean normalized
# reward vs. simulator-sample budget for DRAM memory-controller design
# (DRAMGym, DRAMSys). Means and whisker extents are programmatically
# digitized from the published figure's native-resolution raster (arXiv
# 2306.08888v1 PDF, accessed 2026-07-13; axis-calibration residual <= 0.002
# reward units, reading uncertainty ~ +/-0.003). The paper does not define the
# normalization reference, aggregation population, or statistical meaning of
# its whiskers in the discussion surrounding Figure 7. RL bars are absent in
# the source at the 100 and 1,000-sample budgets and are omitted, not zeroed.
budgets = ["100", "1K", "10K", "100K", "250K"]
agents = [
    ("ACO", COLORS["blue"]),
    ("BO", COLORS["orange"]),
    ("GA", COLORS["green"]),
    ("RL", COLORS["red"]),
    ("RW", COLORS["purple"]),
]
# (mean, whisker low, whisker high) per budget; None = not plotted in source
dram = {
    "ACO": [(0.026, 0.017, 0.037), (0.034, 0.024, 0.046), (0.045, 0.033, 0.058),
            (0.030, 0.021, 0.041), (0.051, 0.039, 0.065)],
    "BO": [(0.046, 0.025, 0.064), (0.112, 0.042, 0.182), (0.112, 0.042, 0.182),
           (0.112, 0.042, 0.182), (0.115, 0.048, 0.182)],
    "GA": [(0.043, 0.025, 0.070), (0.083, 0.053, 0.110), (0.098, 0.070, 0.124),
           (0.097, 0.062, 0.124), (0.107, 0.081, 0.125)],
    "RL": [None, None, (0.007, 0.003, 0.013),
           (0.111, 0.062, 0.208), (0.154, 0.134, 0.168)],
    "RW": [(0.027, 0.019, 0.032), (0.027, 0.014, 0.037), (0.132, 0.112, 0.153),
           (0.132, 0.117, 0.147), (0.132, 0.121, 0.142)],
}

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.7, 2.25))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.105, right=0.985, top=0.83, bottom=0.19)

width = 0.15
for k, (name, color) in enumerate(agents):
    xs, ys, err_lo, err_hi = [], [], [], []
    for i, v in enumerate(dram[name]):
        if v is None:
            continue
        xs.append(i + (k - 2) * width)
        ys.append(v[0])
        err_lo.append(v[0] - v[1])
        err_hi.append(v[2] - v[0])
    ax.bar(xs, ys, width=width * 0.9, color=color, zorder=2, label=name)
    ax.errorbar(xs, ys, yerr=[err_lo, err_hi], fmt="none",
                ecolor=COLORS["ink"], elinewidth=0.7, capsize=1.4,
                capthick=0.7, zorder=3)

ax.set_xticks(range(len(budgets)))
ax.set_xticklabels(budgets)
ax.set_xlabel("simulator-sample budget", fontsize=7)
ax.set_ylabel("mean normalized reward", fontsize=7)
ax.set_ylim(0, 0.23)
ax.set_yticks([0.0, 0.05, 0.10, 0.15, 0.20])
ax.tick_params(axis="both", labelsize=6.5, length=2.5, width=0.6, pad=2)
ax.grid(axis="y", color=COLORS["grid"], linewidth=0.55)
for spine in ["top", "right"]:
    ax.spines[spine].set_visible(False)
ax.spines["left"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
ax.spines["bottom"].set_color(COLORS["ink"])
fig.legend(loc="upper center", ncol=5, fontsize=6.2, frameon=False,
           bbox_to_anchor=(0.53, 1.0), handlelength=1.0, columnspacing=1.2)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

At 10,000 or fewer samples in @fig-ch05-archgym-equal-budget, the paper
describes a random walker as competitive with Bayesian optimization, genetic
algorithms, and ant colony optimization. Reinforcement learning improves
sharply at 100,000 or more samples, while simpler methods remain competitive.
The paper separately reports an interquartile spread of up to 90 percent across
hyperparameter choices in the DRAM task. Those changes mean that an apparent
method advantage can depend on the evaluation budget and tuning effort, not
only on the search family.

The whisker intervals overlap across the figure. In the discussion surrounding
the plot, the paper does not state the normalization reference or aggregation
population for mean normalized reward, and it does not define the whisker
statistic. The plotted values were digitized from the published raster rather
than recomputed from raw results. The figure therefore supports a narrow
within-figure observation about how results change with sample budget, not an
absolute reward interpretation or a precise pairwise ranking. That limitation
is itself an example of why observation meaning belongs in the environment
contract.

The AlphaChip dispute provides a contrasting case in which public comparisons
did not share all of those conditions. Its purpose here is not to settle the
dispute, but to show why the surrounding tool and evaluation setup belongs in
the review record.

::: {.callout-war-story title="Different setups produced different answers"}
**The claim.** The original AlphaChip work and later reassessments reported different placement results
and reached different conclusions about the method's advantage
[@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL].

**The gap.** They used different public and proprietary cases,
baselines, compute budgets, implementations, and evaluation flows
[@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL]. The original authors argued that the reassessments had not run the method as published, while
the reassessments argued that stronger baselines remained competitive in their
settings [@GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed].

**The lesson.** Without one shared setup, a reviewer cannot tell whether a changed result comes
from the method or from the conditions around it. Workload identifiers, tool
versions, seeds, budgets, baselines, and physical checks are part of
the comparison, not incidental implementation details.
:::

The completed SCALE-Sim comparison illustrates the opposite case. Its two arms
use the same simulator version, workload, legal geometries, evaluation budget,
parser, and objective, so another architect can attribute the observed tie to
the evaluated candidates rather than a changed tool setup.

To compare methods fairly, the architect must hold the task, represented state,
evaluation budget, feedback, and outcome rules constant, or document exactly
how they differ. The record must distinguish illegal requests, evaluated
candidates that fail a declared condition, tool-path failures, and results that
require review. ArchGym can be described using the contract fields in
@tbl-environment-contract (@tbl-archgym-mini-contract). The framework supplies
a shared task, action, and simulator interface, but each comparison still has
to define the metric, outcome statuses, dependency and artifact versions, and
review authority. ArchGym does not fill those fields for every comparison. The
architect must define them for the comparison at hand.

| **Contract field** | **What a reviewable ArchGym use needs** |
| --- | --- |
| **Task** | Search or tune a bounded architecture design space under a declared workload and metric. |
| **Action schema** | Architecture parameters, simulator knobs, or mapping choices the automated method is allowed to change. |
| **Observation and reward** | Simulator metrics, their units and scope, and the user-defined reward derived from them. For the Figure 7 comparison, the published plot reports mean normalized reward but does not state the normalization reference, aggregation population, or whisker statistic. |
| **Workload and tool path** | Benchmark inputs, simulator configuration, versioned evaluation scripts, and artifact identifiers. |
| **Outcome handling** | Separate status values for illegal requests, evaluated candidates that fail a declared condition, tool-path failures, and unsupported or inconclusive results. |
| **Evaluation cost** | Approximate turnaround and sample budget for each evaluation regime. |
| **Review and authority** | Independent checks, the reviewer or decision owner for unresolved results, and the strongest conclusion this setup can support. |
| **Limits** | Simplified simulators, cleaner action spaces, and missing industrial constraints can bound what the result proves. |

: **Mapping ArchGym to the environment contract.** ArchGym supplies the shared task, action, and simulator interface. A reviewable comparison still has to fill in the metric meaning, outcome statuses, provenance, and decision rights. {#tbl-archgym-mini-contract tbl-colwidths="[24,66]"}

A single environment can support many methods, including Bayesian optimization,
reinforcement learning, evolutionary search[^fn-ml-evolutionary-search],
surrogate-guided exploration[^fn-ml-surrogate-guided], random search, heuristic
search, or an architect using the environment as an instrumented assistant.
Defining the environment separately makes those comparisons possible.

[^fn-ml-evolutionary-search]: Evolutionary search proposes candidates through mutation, crossover, and selection. It is useful here only when declared validity and rejection checks can identify invalid or weak candidates.

[^fn-ml-surrogate-guided]: Surrogate-guided exploration uses a fast predictor to screen candidate actions while preserving the stronger feedback needed to reject proxy wins.

ArchGym covers bounded tasks through shared interfaces. Industrial design may
add proprietary physical constraints, confidential workloads, tool-license
behavior, workload drift, failed-run capture, and deployment telemetry. Its
action spaces may also be less regular and its feedback slower than the
environments ArchGym exposes. The architect must check whether the shared
environment still represents the intended architecture problem before
extending a result beyond its stated task.

## Stable Interfaces Let Methods Share a Harness

A wrapper written for one experiment can hide assumptions in its scripts. A
stable interface lets several methods work on the same architecture task
without rebuilding those assumptions for each method. Generators, predictors,
optimizers, critics, verifiers, and reviewers can then share the same
action schema, observations, workloads, provenance, and validity rules without
changing the experimental conditions.

Benchmark governance already provides rules, versions, metrics, submission
formats, and comparison conventions that help a community interpret results
[@MattsonEtAl2020MLPerf]. The shared interface must additionally state
what an AI method may try, which actions are invalid, what feedback means, and
how each outcome is handled, including who reviews an unresolved result.
Publishing a script without that contract leaves the important assumptions
private. A maintained interface must also version and revalidate those
assumptions after the first paper or release.

A maintained harness adds shared workloads, versions, permissions, and review
records around the wrapper. It preserves the task and workload versions,
editable and read-only fields, exact commands and observations, cost and
fidelity, seeds, tool versions, artifacts, and the outcome status. It also
records any reviewer recommendation and decision-owner authorization. A
reviewer can then inspect the command that produced a winning score as well as
the failed alternatives, warnings, and conditions that would overturn it.

A compound system may assign generation, prediction, critique, verification,
and coordination to separate components while an architect retains decision
authority. The harness must record which represented state each component
read, what action it took, which inputs and results it relied on, and which
check reviewed its output. Stable fields let those roles share the harness.
Their feedback cost and fidelity determine which combinations are practical.

## Feedback Latency and Fidelity

Every observation returned through an environment must carry its cost and
fidelity.
An analytic proxy may return in milliseconds, a cycle-level simulation may take
minutes or hours, and physical implementation or silicon measurements may
require days to months. If these differences remain hidden, a fast method can
overwhelm an expensive tool path or compare measurements that do not mean the
same thing.

Feedback sources differ by orders of magnitude in turnaround and in how many
samples an evaluation budget can afford. @fig-fidelity-cost-confidence
compares those sources from analytic proxies through deployment observations.
Published tool reports provide measured examples for cycle-level simulation
and physical feedback. The ranges are not universal measurements, and higher
cost does not make an observation automatically authoritative. Each run needs
its tool stage and version, elapsed cost, fidelity, raw observations, and
outcome status. When a run fails, the record should identify which action
triggered it if the cause can be established. A selection policy can then use
those fields to choose the next affordable observation, while a reviewer uses
the fidelity and scope to decide whether it supports a stronger claim.

The cost structure is a multi-fidelity problem familiar in computational
science, where many cheap observations are combined with fewer expensive ones
to make optimization and uncertainty quantification affordable
[^fn-stats-uncertainty-quantification]
[@PeherstorferWillcoxGunzburger2018Multifidelity]. An architecture environment
must expose the observation sources and their costs. The architect must define
when the method should request a more expensive evaluation.

[^fn-stats-uncertainty-quantification]: Uncertainty quantification here means recording where an environment's observations are uncertain or unsupported so a method or reviewer knows when stronger feedback is needed.

```{python}
#| label: fig-fidelity-cost-confidence
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **Feedback sources have different costs and sample budgets:** Low-cost feedback can screen many candidates, while implementation-adjacent observations are scarce. The ordering shows representative turnaround and acquisition cost, not automatic authority or commitment. Each check still needs a declared scope and validation contract. Ink diamonds add published measured anchors. FireSim simulates 1,024 nodes cycle-exactly at 3.4 MHz, under 1,000 times slower than the 3.2 GHz targets it models, where single-server software simulators run at 5 to 100 KIPS [@KarandikarEtAl2018FireSim]. A macro-placement re-evaluation measured 11 hours per design for simulated annealing and 38 to 55 hours for the reinforcement-learning flow, against under 12 hours of expert effort, for one physical-design stage evaluated through a commercial post-route flow [@ChengEtAl2023MacroPlacement].
#| out-width: "92%"
#| fig-alt: "Horizontal range plot comparing representative observation latencies and shrinking sample budgets for feedback regimes from cheap proxies to silicon deployment, without treating cost or fidelity as automatic authority. Ink diamond markers add measured anchors, a FireSim cycle-exact simulation point at just under one thousand times real time on the cycle-level simulation row, and macro-placement turnaround points from eleven to fifty-five hours per design on the physical-feedback row."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style, draw_range_rows, row_axis, top_log_axis

rows = [
    {"display_label": "analytic proxy / surrogate", "display_note": "thousands to millions of checks", "low_seconds": 0.001, "high_seconds": 1, "right_label": "screen candidates", "color": COLORS["blue"]},
    {"display_label": "trace / profile / replay", "display_note": "tens to thousands of slices", "low_seconds": 1, "high_seconds": 3600, "right_label": "characterize workload", "color": COLORS["green"]},
    {"display_label": "cycle-level simulation", "display_note": "tens to hundreds of comparisons", "low_seconds": 60, "high_seconds": 86400, "right_label": "compare candidates", "color": COLORS["orange"]},
    {"display_label": "compiler / runtime measurement", "display_note": "many local measurements; fewer portable claims", "low_seconds": 1, "high_seconds": 3600, "right_label": "validate software path", "color": COLORS["purple"]},
    {"display_label": "RTL / synthesis / physical feedback", "display_note": "handfuls to tens of scarce samples", "low_seconds": 3600, "high_seconds": 1_209_600, "right_label": "implementation evidence", "color": COLORS["red"]},
    {"display_label": "prototype / silicon / deployment", "display_note": "few deployment observations plus telemetry", "low_seconds": 604_800, "high_seconds": 31_536_000, "right_label": "deployment evidence", "color": COLORS["red"]},
]

apply_style()
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.85, 3.75))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.39, right=0.77, top=0.86, bottom=0.16)

row_axis(ax, len(rows))
top_log_axis(
    ax,
    xlim=(5e-4, 7e7),
    xticks=[1e-3, 1, 3600, 604800, 31536000],
    xticklabels=["1 ms", "1 s", "1 h", "1 wk", "1 yr"],
    xlabel="representative observation latency",
)
draw_range_rows(ax, rows, low_key="low_seconds", high_key="high_seconds", label_x=-0.90, right_x=1.03, label_fontsize=6.5, note_fontsize=5.3, right_fontsize=5.9)

# Measured anchors (source receipt:
# data/source-receipts/chapter5-measured-feedback-anchors.csv).
# FireSim ISCA 2018 [@KarandikarEtAl2018FireSim]: 1,024-node cycle-exact
# simulation at 3.4 MHz vs. 3.2 GHz targets, "less than 1,000x slowdown over
# real-time" -> ~941 s to observe one second of target execution; the paper
# also gives per-node 10s-100s MHz and 5-100 KIPS for single-server software
# simulators (rates only, cited in the caption, no latency marker).
# Cheng et al. ISPD 2023 [@ChengEtAl2023MacroPlacement], Table IV, CT-Ariane:
# macro placement evaluated through a commercial post-route flow, SA 11.13 h,
# RL 37.89-55.16 h, human expert <12 h per design. Accessed 2026-07-13.
# The Verilator anchor is dropped: its only absolute measured rate is outside
# the bibliography.
firesim_x = 3.2e9 / 3.4e6  # ~941 s per second of target execution
sa_x = 11.13 * 3600
rl_lo_x, rl_hi_x = 37.89 * 3600, 55.16 * 3600
# The RL 38-55 h range is anchored by ink diamonds at both endpoints so every
# measured anchor uses the same diamond vocabulary as the note box.
ax.scatter([firesim_x, sa_x, rl_lo_x, rl_hi_x], [2, 4, 4, 4], marker="D",
           s=11, color=COLORS["ink"], zorder=4)
ax.text(firesim_x, 2.34,
        "FireSim, 1,024 nodes, cycle-exact:\n<1,000$\\times$ real time",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=5.3, color=COLORS["muted"],
        linespacing=1.15, clip_on=False)
ax.text(3.2e4, 4.34,
        "macro placement, measured:\nSA 11 h, RL 38$-$55 h, expert <12 h",
        ha="center", va="center", fontsize=5.3, color=COLORS["muted"],
        linespacing=1.15, clip_on=False)

add_note_box(fig, "Representative ranges only; ink diamonds mark published measured anchors; authority depends on scope, validation, and the decision contract.", xywh=(0.13, 0.035, 0.74, 0.085), fontsize=5.5)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

Read @fig-fidelity-cost-confidence from top to bottom. Cheap proxies and traces
can screen many candidates, while cycle-level simulation, RTL and physical
implementation, and deployment observations support far fewer evaluations.
The rows describe acquisition cost and practical sample budgets, not a ladder
of automatic authority. An architect may use a carefully validated simulator
to settle a narrow performance comparison, while an expensive physical run may
still be irrelevant to a different workload or claim.

The measured markers ground the cycle-level and physical-design rows. FireSim
can provide cycle-exact feedback at a much higher rate
than single-server software simulation, yet it still runs below target-system
speed. A post-route macro-placement evaluation consumed eleven to fifty-five
hours of tool time per design. An AI method can spend thousands of cheap proxy
calls, but only a handful of evaluations at that physical-design cost. The
environment must expose those differences so the architect can choose a
suitable method and decide when a scarcer check is worth its cost.

@tbl-sample-cost-regimes introduced the broad cost differences among
observation sources. To make those ranges usable inside an environment, the
harness must record what each source returned and apply the same four outcome
statuses used throughout this chapter. @tbl-feedback-latency-fidelity shows how
those statuses appear at different fidelity levels.

| **Observation source** | **Cost / latency intuition** | **Environment record** | **How the status should be recorded** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Analytic proxy or learned surrogate | Milliseconds to seconds; many checks are possible. | Model and version, input domain, raw estimate, uncertainty, and calibration state. | **Unsupported or inconclusive** when the input is outside calibrated support; **candidate rejected after evaluation** when a supported estimate misses a declared bound. |
| Trace, profile, or replay | Seconds to hours depending on capture, replay, and privacy filtering. | Trace origin, workload and software version, slice rule, coverage, and redactions. | **Unsupported or inconclusive** for a stale or incomplete trace; **candidate rejected after evaluation** for behavior that fails on a valid trace. |
| Cycle-level simulation | Minutes to days depending on detail and workload. | Simulator and configuration, seed, warm-up rule, statistics, warnings, and elapsed time. | **Illegal before execution** for a request outside the schema; **tool-path failure** for a crash, timeout, or broken setup. |
| Compiler/runtime measurement | Build and profiling may take seconds to hours; target replay or tuning may take longer. | Toolchain, flags, generated code, runtime path, tests, counters, and host state. | **Candidate rejected after evaluation** for an artifact that fails a declared build or correctness check; **tool-path failure** for broken build infrastructure. |
| RTL, synthesis, or physical feedback | Hours to weeks as timing, power, congestion, and signoff enter. | EDA stage, constraints, libraries, reports, warnings, waivers, host state, and hashes. | **Candidate rejected after evaluation** for a declared timing, power, or rule violation; **tool-path failure** for a license, queue, timeout, or tool problem. |
| Prototype, silicon, or deployment telemetry | High setup cost; field observations may span weeks or years. | Hardware revision, rollout state, telemetry schema, workload mix, incidents, and collection gaps. | **Candidate rejected after evaluation** for a declared regression; **tool-path failure** for a deployment or measurement problem. |

: **Observation sources have different costs and outcome meanings:** Representative ranges are project-dependent. The environment records enough context to compare observations and to separate candidate outcomes from tool-path failures. {#tbl-feedback-latency-fidelity tbl-colwidths="[19,22,32,25]"}

The last column of @tbl-feedback-latency-fidelity applies the four statuses to
concrete tool results. A cache request outside the legal range is illegal before
execution. A valid configuration that exceeds a declared bandwidth limit is a
candidate rejected after evaluation. A simulator crash caused by a broken
build is a tool-path failure. A proxy result outside its calibrated range is
unsupported or inconclusive. The harness should also retain a cheap proxy and a
stronger contradictory check as separate results. When the proxy says "yes"
and a higher-fidelity tool says "no," the harness should retain the mismatch
rather than average it away.

Tool results can also attribute a software weakness to the hardware candidate.
A candidate can look weak because the schedule, tiling, vectorization,
layout, or runtime path is poor, not because the architecture is poor. That is
a false negative created by the software side of the environment. A claimed
vector capability requires evidence that the compiler and runtime can
generate a credible path to use it; the existence of an ISA feature is insufficient.

The environment should return each observation with its source, cost,
provenance, fidelity, and outcome status. A method can use this information to
decide which observation to request next, including in active
learning.[^fn-ml-active-learning] The reviewer still determines which claims
the returned observation can support.

[^fn-ml-active-learning]: Active learning selects which scarce simulator run, synthesis run, deployment measurement, or expert assessment to request next [@Settles2009ActiveLearning]. The environment must provide the selectable actions and record the resulting call.

```{=latex}
\FloatBarrier
```

## Proxy Gaming and the Simulator Case

Low-fidelity models can omit behavior that an aggressive search method learns
to exploit. Simulators, analytical models, profilers, and compiler cost
models are not neutral oracles. They encode workload choices, warm-up rules,
timing models, memory-system assumptions, compiler defaults, branch predictor
state, cache initialization, interconnect models, and sampling choices. A
method that searches aggressively can discover weaknesses in those assumptions
just as easily as it can discover a good architecture candidate.

*Proxy mismatch* is the divergence between what a cheap stand-in metric rewards
and what the architectural objective requires. When a method optimizes against
that stand-in, the mismatch can produce the failure described by Goodhart's law,
in which a measure becomes unreliable as a target
[@Goodhart1975MonetaryManagement; @Strathern1997Goodhart]. For a simulator,
proxy mismatch is the gap between the behavior the environment reports and the behavior that would matter at
the next stronger fidelity level, such as a more detailed simulator, synthesis,
physical design, emulation, silicon, or deployment. The result may be more
dangerous than an inaccurate estimate because an optimizer can prefer the
candidate that exploits the omission. A
mapping optimizer may exploit a memory model that omits contention. A compiler
autotuner may win by relying on a backend assumption that changes under a
different target. A hardware generator may improve a cycle-level metric while
creating timing, congestion, or verification problems that the current
environment cannot see. For example, an automated memory partitioner might find
a partition that hides latency in the simulator's memory model, only for
place-and-route to expose severe congestion when thousands of wires must cross
the die, preventing timing closure. A workload harness may reward one benchmark
version while hiding drift in the software stack.
A successful simulator run shows that the configuration executed under the
model. It does not show that the model covers every behavior relevant to the
claim. The contract must state which illegal states, unsupported
configurations, and silent out-of-model behaviors the wrapper can detect and
report.

When a method actively exploits this mismatch, the failure is called
*specification gaming* or *reward hacking* in machine-learning safety
[@AmodeiEtAl2016ConcreteProblems]. A candidate can improve a proxy by choosing
an unsupported parameter corner, relying on a stale workload slice, or shifting
cost into a compiler/runtime path the current harness does not measure. The
harness should run invalid-action checks, baseline replay, and sensitivity
tests. The architect should define when a proxy win or inconclusive check
requires stronger tool feedback or reviewer inspection. Calibration evidence
must show where the proxy preserves a ranking and where a stronger check
overturns it.

Architecture-tool integrity can also fail when a trace is poisoned, a baseline
or dependency changes without being recorded, or a log or tool result is
tampered with. Version and artifact hashes should expose those changes, while
targeted red-team checks[^fn-ml-red-teaming] should confirm that the harness
detects them before comparing runs.

A simulator-backed harness should record warm-up policy, execution- versus
trace-driven mode, random seeds, sampled regions, versioned workloads,
configuration files, and unsupported states. Rejection tests should also look
for proxy gaming through sanity constraints on bandwidth and latency,
sensitivity studies, baseline replay, and comparison with a stronger model.
These checks give the architect a basis for deciding which conclusions the
simulator output can support and where those conclusions stop.

[^fn-ml-red-teaming]: Here, a red-team check deliberately changes a trace, baseline, dependency, or tool record to test whether the harness detects the change before using the output as evidence.

## Building a Small Architecture Environment

An architect can begin with one small task. The AI method proposes actions
through a declared schema, the tools return recorded observations, and the
harness records whether a request was illegal, an evaluated candidate failed a
declared condition, the tool path failed, or the result requires review. The
task should be narrow enough that its actions, observations, constraints, and
rejection conditions can be stated without private knowledge.

The steps below make that tool path usable in a reviewable architecture
comparison.

**Environment recipe.** The following steps construct a small Architecture 2.0 environment whose action rules, observations, and outcome paths can be tested.

1. Define the task in architectural language, such as workload characterization, cache exploration, accelerator parameter search, compiler/runtime tuning, chiplet partitioning, reliability analysis, or design-review critique.
2. Choose the representation the AI method will read and write, such as a configuration file, architecture description, graph, trace record, report, RTL fragment, or test bench.
3. Define the action schema by stating which fields the AI method may change, which are read-only, which combinations are legal, and which changes require approval from a named architect or decision owner.
4. Wrap the tool path, such as a simulator, compiler, profiler, RTL flow, EDA stage, runtime system, benchmark harness, or telemetry pipeline.
5. Define the observations and their meaning, including metrics, traces, logs, warnings, errors, generated artifacts, units, aggregation, denominator, normalization, preferred direction, uncertainty, valid scope, cost, and fidelity.
6. Define four outcome statuses. Record an illegal request before execution, a candidate rejected after a valid evaluation, a tool-path failure, or an unsupported or inconclusive result that requires review.
7. Preserve run and provenance records, including tool versions, seeds, workload versions, failed runs, rejected alternatives, and the reason for each outcome.
8. State which checks may route a result automatically to the next tool stage, what the architect reviews, and who may accept, reject, defer, or escalate a candidate or claim.

Once built, the readiness test is simple. Could a second method, student, or
research group act inside the same harness and interpret its observations
without private knowledge from the original author? Could a rejected candidate
remain understandable six months later after tool versions, workload revisions,
and scripts have changed? If the answer is no, the project may still contain a
useful wrapper, but it has not yet produced a maintainable Architecture 2.0
environment.

These steps are sufficient for a small but useful environment. The first useful
environment for a new subfield is often narrow. A bounded workload, legal
action space, documented wrapper, and recorded outcomes let a method propose
candidates and let a reviewer reconstruct what happened to each one.

A narrow SCALE-Sim study applies this recipe with one workload, one simulator
configuration, one legal geometry family, two equal-budget arms, and one
mechanism probe. That contract is enough to check candidate legality, preserve
tool costs and results, and interpret a narrow null comparison without implying
coverage of the entire accelerator design process.

The same contract applies to a single hardware prefetcher. Its actions are
prefetch depth and pattern. A cycle-level simulator returns cheap feedback on
cache hit rate and memory bandwidth. Any configuration that breaches a
bandwidth or correctness bound is rejected. The size of the artifact does not
determine whether the environment is complete. Legal actions, observable
feedback, and explicit rejection criteria do.
It is complete only after it names the workload trace, simulator version,
rejected configurations, failed tool calls, proxy limits, and the conditions
that require the architect to seek a correctness or traffic review instead of
acting on hit rate alone.

A change to a parser, tool default, workload version, outcome status,
or action schema can alter the comparison even when the AI method is unchanged.
Replay representative candidates after such a change, and do not combine the
old and new results until an architect has explained any difference.

## Keeping the Environment Valid as Tools Change {#sec-environment-lifecycle}

An environment remains useful only while its workloads, legal actions, and tool
outputs still match the architecture question. If the workload is wrong, the
action space omits the important decision, the simulator hides a constraint,
the proxy is uncalibrated, or the logging drops rejected candidates or failed
runs, an AI method may simply become faster at producing results that do not
answer the question.

To preserve that link, check the parts of the environment that can go stale.

- **Workload:** The inputs, distributions, and software stack match the
  intended use.
- **Actions:** The AI method can change the right architectural variables
  without violating hidden constraints.
- **Observations:** Returned metrics identify their source and intended scope so
  a reviewer can judge whether they still answer the architecture question.
- **Feedback cost:** Cost and latency are known before the method or
  architect selects the next evaluation.
- **Provenance:** Recorded tool versions, inputs, assumptions, and outputs
  support audit. Replay additionally requires executable bindings to the
  declared environment and procedure.
- **Outcomes:** Each result uses one of four statuses: illegal before
  execution, candidate rejected after evaluation, tool-path failure, or
  unsupported or inconclusive and sent for review.

The following checkpoint applies these requirements before a tool result is used.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Before Using Tool Output as Evidence"}
Before using an environment's output to judge an AI-generated candidate, confirm:

- the workload, action space, and observations still match the bounded
  architecture question;
- the record states each observation's source, units, aggregation,
  normalization, uncertainty, valid scope, and the cost and latency of another
  evaluation;
- versions, assumptions, and rejected candidates are recorded;
- each result has one of the four declared outcome statuses, and the record
  names who reviews an unsupported or inconclusive result.
:::

Ordinary lifecycle practice keeps those parts aligned over time. Versioning,
monitoring, drift detection, rollback, and ownership records are familiar from
MLOps[^fn-systems-mlops], benchmark governance, and reliable service operation.
Applied here, they keep an environment's workloads, tools, action schemas, and
outcome meanings from changing silently after the first paper or release.

[^fn-systems-mlops]: MLOps maintains deployed learning systems through practices such as versioning, monitoring, drift detection, rollback, and ownership records [@SculleyEtAl2015Hidden; @BeyerEtAl2016SRE]. The same practices help keep architecture harnesses comparable as their workloads and tools change.

@tbl-environment-lifecycle maps common sources of drift to the information an
architect must preserve and the response each change may require. Recording
ordinary version and workload changes prevents them from silently changing
what an observation means.

| **Drift source** | **What to record** | **Risk if ignored** | **What to do** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Workload and benchmark updates | Versions, scenarios, excluded inputs, telemetry shifts, and coverage changes. | An AI method keeps optimizing a stale or unrepresentative slice. | Re-run coverage checks and weaken or invalidate affected claims. |
| Tool and model changes | Simulator, compiler, EDA tool, model, prompt, policy, dependency, seed, configuration, artifact hashes, and signatures. | Results become incomparable across runs without a clear cause, or a changed dependency silently alters the evidence source. | Recheck affected results and preserve old run records. |
| Action-space changes | New knobs, deprecated knobs, invalid combinations, and permission boundaries. | A method proposes actions the environment no longer supports. | Update validity checks and record invalid-action traces. |
| Observation and review rules | Fidelity labels, escalation thresholds, signoff checks, waivers, and reviewer decisions. | A proxy keeps being used after the claim has moved beyond its validated scope. | Recalibrate the rule and require review appropriate to the new claim. |
| Decision ownership | Decision owner, review date, redaction boundary, and escalation path. | No named person is responsible for the decision. | Name who may approve, defer, reject, or escalate the proposed action. |

: **Maintaining the environment preserves validity over time:** Version workloads, tools, actions, observation and review rules, and ownership so that drift does not silently change what an AI-assisted result means. {#tbl-environment-lifecycle tbl-colwidths="[18,25,25,22]"}

Each row shows how an unrecorded change can make old results incomparable or
leave no one responsible for rechecking them. Versioning and revalidation show
which runs and claims need attention.

## Open Research Questions

Five parts of making this contract executable remain difficult across real
tool flows.

1. **What is the smallest environment contract that lets an AI method act
without private knowledge?** @tbl-environment-contract identifies common
fields, but their required detail varies across simulators, compilers,
commercial EDA flows, and deployment systems. Research should identify which
omissions actually cause an independent method to take illegal actions,
misread feedback, or produce a result another architect cannot review.

2. **Can AI infer a candidate environment contract from legacy tool traces
without treating accidental behavior as a rule?** Legacy EDA flows and
simulators expose warnings, timeouts, partial artifacts, and unstructured logs
rather than clean APIs. Inferred action schemas and outcome rules must capture
stable tool behavior while leaving uncertain cases visible for an architect to
correct.

3. **How should ambiguous outcomes be attributed after an AI-generated action?**
Illegal actions, malformed inputs, tool crashes, proxy warnings, architectural
rejections, and inconclusive runs require different responses. A timeout, for
example, could reflect an extreme candidate or unstable infrastructure. The
classification must avoid blaming the candidate for a broken tool path while
preserving cases that require an architect or reviewer.

4. **When can high-fidelity failures constrain a cheaper AI search?** Routing
congestion, IR drop, timing violations, and design-rule failures may reveal
physical limits that a simulator or surrogate cannot see. Transferring a
redacted constraint into a cheaper environment could identify weak candidates
earlier, but the constraint may not hold across workloads, floorplans, or
technology nodes. @tbl-eda-stage-contract shows the stages across which that
scope must remain visible.

5. **Which prior AI-assisted results must be rechecked after an environment
changes?** A new workload, simulator, model, prompt, dependency, or review rule
can affect some earlier claims without invalidating all of them. A useful
system would trace each change to the actions, observations, and claims it can
alter, then present the affected results to the responsible architect or
reviewer.

## Conclusion

An architecture environment makes a tool flow usable by an AI method and
reviewable by another architect. It tells the method what it may change and
tells the architect how to interpret what the tool returns. That requires the
workload and fixed context, action schema, observation meaning, cost and
fidelity, and provenance. The four outcomes are illegal before execution,
candidate rejected after evaluation, tool-path failure, and unsupported or
inconclusive pending review.

The contract remains reliable only while the workload, permitted actions,
tools, and observation meanings still match the architecture question. When
one changes, versioning and provenance show which results need to be rechecked
and make representative runs replayable. A one-time wrapper may support one
experiment. A maintained environment lets the next architect determine
whether the harness still answers the same question.

::: {.callout-carry-forward}
- **Carry forward:** An environment specifies legal actions, observation meaning, provenance, and four outcome paths so another method can act in the same harness.
- **Reader test:** Could another method act in the same harness, and could another architect interpret its results, without private knowledge from the original author?
- **Up next:** Once the environment defines what action and feedback mean, @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization can match method roles to the available feedback.
:::
